input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin system is a serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, featuring components such as a permanent magnet synchronous motor and gear transmission, designed for high-precision industrial applications requiring dynamic motion control and real-time monitoring capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for system structure modeling, utilizing methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs define component and connector perspectives, describing system components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavior modeling is achieved via MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines (v1.7), integrated with simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose programming languages such as C++ and Java.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Shadow Reference Model (Fig. 1) defines a component-based architecture where DataTrace components aggregate and abstract contextual data from Source entities (e.g., sensor streams), linked to physical Assets, with Processing components handling data transformation. The system operates as a one-way data flow from physical Asset to digital representation, structured via UML class diagram elements including DataTrace, Source, Asset, and Processing, with no autonomous feedback to the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models include geometric representations via SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs), behavioral models using SysML state machines and MontiArc Automaton, and analytical models through MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java). Data sources are derived from these modeling tools and simulation environments. Data management involves integrating heterogeneous models across different notations and tools, as per MBSE methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT), addressing stakeholder perspectives through component-based architectures defined in ADLs such as AADL, ACME, and MontiArc.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor time series data (e.g., input current and output angle) is streamed via MQTT protocol in a one-way data flow from physical to digital components as part of the digital shadow reference model.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: No virtual-to-physical interaction occurs as the digital shadow is a passive entity that neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system, consistent with the defined one-way data flow characteristic.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for transmitting sensor data between physical and digital components; no additional network topology, bandwidth, latency, or security details are specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system complies with industry standards including SysML [17] for systems engineering modeling, AADL [24], ACME [23], and MontiArc [25, 26] for architecture description languages, and methodologies such as RFLP [18, 19], OOSEM [20], SPES [21], and SMArDT [22] for handling system complexity across abstraction levels. These standards ensure interoperability and structured development across requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical layers.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: Accurately identifies the specific robot model (MABI MAX 100), its axes, and key components like the permanent magnet synchronous motor and gear transmission. It correctly notes the application in high-precision tasks like machining. However, it misses specific details present in the text such as the Siemens SINUMERIK controller and specific encoder configurations, which prevents a perfect score.
quotes: serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes
suitable for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text describes the construction and purpose of the digital shadow (e.g., 'improve robot motion accuracy', 'visualization in a dashboard'), but it does not explicitly define or categorize specific 'services' (monitoring, optimization, prediction, etc.) with target users and capabilities as requested by the characteristic description. The text focuses on the implementation methodology rather than a service-oriented description. Therefore, the claim that this specific characteristic is not in the document is correct.

The description is highly detailed and accurately reflects the background section (II.A), listing specific tools and methodologies including 'Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)', 'SysML', 'RFLP', 'OOSEM', 'MontiArc', and 'MathWorks Simulink'.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The document mentions the use of 'live data' and 'real-time' representations, but it does not provide specific technical details regarding synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs as defined by the characteristic. The extraction 'Not in Document' is correct as the relevant specific details are absent.

The document describes the creation of a single digital shadow for one robot axis. It mentions multiple data series and model types, but does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, centralized vs decentralized architectures, or coordination between twins. The extraction 'Not in Document' is correct.

The description accurately captures the Digital Shadow Reference Model described in Section II.B. It correctly notes the 'one-way data flow', the components ('DataTrace', 'Source', 'Asset', 'Processing'), and the lack of 'autonomous feedback'. It is supported by the text: 'A digital shadow is characterized by “an automated one-way data flow between the state of an existing physical object and a digital object”' and 'neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system'.

The document mentions the implementation of Asset Administration Shells (AAS) and MQTT for data streaming, but these are used for the internal operation of the digital shadow (vertical integration with the robot). It does not describe integration patterns with external systems, other digital twins, or enterprise systems. The extraction 'Not in Document' is correct.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the detailed list of modeling languages, tools, and methodologies described in the 'Modeling Software and Systems' section of the text. It correctly identifies the use of SysML IBDs for structure, MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines for behavior, and Simulink/C++/Java for detailed analysis. It also correctly lists the MBSE methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT) and ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc) as well as the challenge of integrating heterogeneous models. Supported by quotes: 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML', 'methodologies... e.g., RFLP..., OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT', and 'ADLs... e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc... or SysML IBDs'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source document explicitly describes physical acting components. The first axis of the robot includes a permanent magnet synchronous motor and gearboxes (gear transmission and cycloidal gear) that drive the axis. Quotes: 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor with the current I_M as input and torque τ_M as output. To achieve the necessary low gear ratio, a gear transmission is first used... followed by a cycloidal gear.'

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source document mentions sensing components. There are two position encoders per drive (motor and secondary) to reduce backlash and positional uncertainty. Additionally, sensor data streams (input current and output angle) are accessible via MQTT. Quotes: 'There are two position encoders per drive. The motor encoder is mounted directly on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox.' 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT. These are the input current and the output angle.'

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source document discusses model fidelity and validity considerations. It describes the challenges of modeling friction accurately due to factors like load, temperature, and time. It also mentions combining white-box and black-box models for better accuracy and the need for continuous model improvement during operation. Quotes: 'Industrial robots often experience significant inaccuracies in their movements due to joint friction, which is challenging to model accurately as it depends on various factors such as load, temperature, and time.' 'Existing research falls short in providing methods for continuously improving both white- and black-box friction models during operation.'

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions 'development phase' regarding modeling (Section II.A) and discusses continuous model improvement 'over the operating time of the robot' (Section I). However, it does not describe specific lifecycle phases (e.g., design, deployment, disposal), transition management between phases, or lifecycle governance approaches as defined in the characteristic description. Since the required details are missing, the claim 'Not in Document' is correct.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source documents describe a methodology for creating 'Digital Shadows' (Section II.C and IV.A) involving steps like 'Problem identification', 'Asset and model analysis', and 'Data identification'. It also mentions 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification' for model improvement. However, the rubric specifically asks for 'engineering and development processes' including quality assurance, testing, validation, version control, and evolution management for the *Digital Twin*. The paper focuses on a specific method for *Digital Shadows* and does not detail the broader software engineering practices or version control strategies for the Digital Twin itself. Therefore, stating it is 'Not in Document' is valid based on the strict requirements of the rubric.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The sources state the digital shadow 'enable[s] the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction' and 'improve the robot’s motion accuracy' (Abstract). It also mentions 'integrated visualization within a unified dashboard' (Section I). However, the characteristic requires details on 'decision support capabilities', 'analytics and processing capabilities', 'decision making processes', and 'confidence measures'. While the system provides insights via visualization and friction identification, it does not describe explicit decision-making processes, advanced analytics capabilities beyond simulation, or confidence measures. Thus, the claim that the characteristic is not present is correct.

The extraction lists specific standards and methodologies used in the system. This is fully supported by Section II.A of the source text: 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML [17]... There exist different methodologies on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP... OOSEM... SPES... or SMArDT... To describe the functional and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used... e.g., ACME... AADL... MontiArc...'. The extraction accurately captures these standards and their purpose.
